bytes.topic: REST — resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, idempotency
bytes.title: REST API Design — Resource Naming, Verbs, Status Codes, and Idempotency Done Right
bytes.body: REST resource URLs should be nouns, never verbs. Use `/orders/42/items` not `/getOrderItems`. Collections are plural; singletons are singular. HTTP verbs carry the action: GET (safe + idempotent), PUT (idempotent, full replace), PATCH (partial update, not guaranteed idempotent), POST (non-idempotent, creates subordinate resource), DELETE (idempotent). Idempotency means calling the same request N times produces the same server state as calling it once — critical for retry logic. PUT `/orders/42` with the same body twice must not create a second order. POST `/orders` will. Status codes must be precise: 201 Created (with Location header) on POST success, 200 OK for GET/PUT responses with a body, 204 No Content for DELETE or PUT with no response body, 409 Conflict when a resource already exists, 422 Unprocessable Entity for valid JSON with invalid business rules, 404 only when the resource genuinely doesn't exist. Never return 200 with an error payload — that breaks every HTTP client and monitoring tool.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Correct REST endpoint design

# Resource naming: nouns, plural, hierarchical
GET    /users/99/orders          # list orders for user 99
GET    /users/99/orders/42       # fetch single order
POST   /users/99/orders          # create order (non-idempotent)
PUT    /users/99/orders/42       # full replace (idempotent)
PATCH  /users/99/orders/42       # partial update
DELETE /users/99/orders/42       # delete (idempotent)

# Status code contract
# POST   → 201 Created  + Location: /users/99/orders/42
# PUT    → 200 OK       (body) or 204 No Content
# DELETE → 204 No Content
# PATCH  → 200 OK       (updated resource)
# 409    → duplicate resource; 422 → invalid business rule

bytes.topic: GraphQL — schema design, resolvers, N+1 with DataLoader, subscriptions
bytes.title: REST to GraphQL — Schema Design, Resolvers, N+1, and Subscriptions
bytes.body:
GraphQL flips REST's resource-per-endpoint model into a single `/graphql` endpoint driven by a typed SDL schema. Where REST exposes `GET /users/:id/posts`, GraphQL lets clients declare exactly the fields needed — no over-fetching, no underfetching.

Each SDL field maps to a resolver function — think of it as a per-field route handler. `Query.users` resolves the list; `User.posts` resolves the nested relation. This composability is where REST developers trip: nested resolvers fire once per parent node.

The N+1 problem: 100 users → 100 `User.posts` resolver calls → 100 separate queries. DataLoader fixes this with tick-window batching — it collects all `userId` keys within a single request tick, then fires one `WHERE user_id = ANY($1)`.

Subscriptions replace REST polling. Define a `Subscription` type in SDL, publish via `pubSub.publish('POST_ADDED', payload)`, and clients connect over WebSocket — no more `GET /feed?since=` loops.

Critical migration gotcha: REST auth middleware on a single route doesn't translate. Apply JWT verification in the context factory (`context: ({ req }) => ({ user: verifyJWT(req) })`), not at the HTTP layer — otherwise unauthenticated introspection queries bypass your guards entirely.

bytes.topic: gRPC — protobuf schemas, streaming types, when to use over REST
bytes.title: REST vs gRPC — Protobuf Schemas, Streaming Types, and When to Switch
bytes.body:
REST gives you JSON over HTTP/1.1 — human-readable, but costly at scale. gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (protobuf) over HTTP/2, cutting payload size by 60–80% and enabling multiplexed, bidirectional streams with no polling hacks.

Your `.proto` file is the OpenAPI equivalent, but enforced at compile time — no more schema drift between client and server. Fields are typed and numbered; renaming a field doesn't break old clients as long as the field number stays the same.

gRPC exposes four call types REST can't cleanly model:
- **Unary**: direct drop-in for REST GET/POST
- **Server-streaming**: live feeds, log tailing (replaces SSE)
- **Client-streaming**: bulk uploads, telemetry ingestion
- **Bidirectional**: real-time collaboration, chat

Choose gRPC over REST when: you control both ends (internal services), latency is critical, or you need true streaming without WebSocket complexity. Stick with REST for public-facing APIs — browser gRPC requires `grpc-web` plus a proxy (Envoy or grpc-gateway), which erases the simplicity win.
bytes.code_snippet:
import grpc
import order_pb2, order_pb2_grpc

channel = grpc.insecure_channel("localhost:50051")
stub = order_pb2_grpc.OrderServiceStub(channel)

# Unary — REST GET equivalent
response = stub.GetOrder(order_pb2.OrderRequest(order_id="ord_123"))
print(response.status, response.amount)

# Server-streaming — no clean REST equivalent without SSE
for update in stub.StreamOrders(order_pb2.OrderRequest(order_id="ord_123")):
    print(update.status)  # receives each message as server pushes it

bytes.topic: tRPC — end-to-end type safety, procedure types, React Query integration
bytes.title: REST vs tRPC — End-to-End Type Safety Without the Schema Sync Tax
bytes.body:
tRPC eliminates the type-safety gap that plagues REST APIs. Where REST forces you to manually maintain OpenAPI schemas or write separate client-side type definitions, tRPC infers types directly from your router — no codegen, no drift.

Procedures come in three forms: `query` (GET-equivalent, React Query-cached), `mutation` (POST/PUT/DELETE-equivalent), and `subscription` (WebSocket-backed). Each accepts a Zod input schema; the return type flows to the client automatically via the shared `AppRouter` type.

React Query integration is built-in, not bolted on. `trpc.user.byId.useQuery({ id })` wraps `useQuery` under the hood — you get cache keys, stale-time, and `trpc.user.byId.invalidate()` without extra wiring. Mutations use `useMutation` with identical ergonomics.

The constraint: tRPC demands a TypeScript monorepo or shared package so server and client share the `AppRouter` type. For public REST APIs consumed externally, OpenAPI stays the right call. For internal full-stack TypeScript apps, tRPC eliminates an entire class of runtime bugs that no amount of REST discipline can prevent.

bytes.topic: WebSockets — connection lifecycle, heartbeat, reconnection, rooms
bytes.title: REST + WebSockets — Connection Lifecycle, Heartbeat, and Rooms in Node.js
bytes.body:
WebSockets upgrade an HTTP connection to a persistent, full-duplex channel. Your REST API handles the handshake: client sends `GET` with `Upgrade: websocket`, server responds `101 Switching Protocols`, and the TCP socket is handed off — HTTP is done. From there, frames flow bidirectionally with no polling overhead.

Heartbeats prevent zombie connections. The `ws` library exposes native ping/pong frames: server pings every 30s, client auto-replies with pong. If `ws.isAlive` is still `false` on the next tick, call `ws.terminate()` — not `ws.close()`, which requires a clean handshake your dead client can't complete.

Rooms are just a `Map<WebSocket, string>`. On `join`, store the socket keyed to a room ID. On `broadcast`, iterate `wss.clients`, filter by room, and send only to live sockets with `readyState === WebSocket.OPEN`. No external broker needed at MVP scale.

Reconnection lives client-side: exponential backoff starting at 1s, cap at 30s, reset on successful `open`. Preserve a `clientId` across reconnects so server-side state (room membership, subscriptions) can be restored without treating it as a new session.

bytes.topic: OpenAPI — spec-first vs code-first, generating clients, documentation
bytes.title: REST + OpenAPI — Spec-First vs Code-First, and Generating Typed Clients
bytes.body:
Spec-first means authoring `openapi.yaml` before any server code — your REST contract drives everything. `openapi-generator` produces server stubs and typed clients from it; frontend and backend teams work in parallel against a stable interface. Code-first flips the flow: annotate your REST routes (Swashbuckle in .NET, FastAPI's decorator system in Python) and derive the spec at runtime from your implementation.

Spec-first wins on contract clarity and team coordination; code-first wins on velocity. The real payoff in either approach is client generation. `openapi-typescript` turns an OpenAPI 3.1 spec into a fully typed `paths` interface — every REST endpoint's path params, query params, request body, and response shape are statically typed. Pair it with `openapi-fetch` and you get type-safe fetch calls with zero hand-written wrappers or manual `Response.json()` casting.

Documentation is essentially free: Swagger UI and Redoc render interactive explorers directly from your spec, including auth schemes and example payloads. With spec-first, treat `openapi.yaml` like production code — versioned in git, reviewed in PRs, never hand-edited after a generation run.

bytes.topic: API Versioning — URL vs header versioning, backward compatibility strategy
bytes.title: REST API Versioning — URL Path vs. Header Strategy and Backward Compatibility
bytes.body:
REST APIs expose two dominant versioning strategies. URL path versioning (/v1/users, /v2/users) is explicit, cacheable, and easy to route at the gateway level — but pollutes your URI namespace and violates the REST constraint that URIs should identify resources, not versions. Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v2+json or a custom X-API-Version header) keeps URIs stable and aligns with HTTP content negotiation semantics, but breaks browser-based testing and complicates caching with Vary headers.

For backward compatibility, additive changes (new fields, new optional query params) are safe without bumping versions. Breaking changes — removing fields, changing data types, altering response shapes — require a new version. Use a sunset header (Sunset: Sat, 01 Jan 2027 00:00:00 GMT) on deprecated endpoints to give clients a migration window. Never silently remove a version; return 410 Gone after the sunset date. In practice, most REST APIs ship URL versioning for its operational simplicity: reverse proxies, API gateways, and SDK generators all handle /v1 vs /v2 routing with zero custom middleware.
bytes.code_snippet:
# URL versioning — explicit, gateway-friendly
GET /v1/users/42          # stable legacy contract
GET /v2/users/42          # new shape (e.g. name split into first_name/last_name)

# Header versioning — stable URIs, content-negotiated
GET /users/42
Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v2+json

# Sunset signal on a deprecated v1 endpoint
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Sunset: Sat, 01 Jan 2027 00:00:00 GMT
Deprecation: true
Link: <https://api.example.com/v2/users/42>; rel="successor-version"

bytes.topic: Authentication — OAuth2, JWT in headers, API keys, mTLS
bytes.title: REST API Authentication — OAuth2, JWT, API Keys, and mTLS Explained
bytes.body:
REST APIs are stateless by design, so every request must carry its own proof of identity. The four dominant schemes each fit different trust boundaries.

**API Keys** are simplest — a static secret sent via `Authorization: ApiKey <token>` or a custom header like `X-API-Key`. Fine for server-to-server, terrible for public clients since rotation is manual and there's no expiry semantics.

**JWT Bearer tokens** (RFC 7519) travel in `Authorization: Bearer <token>`. The server validates the signature locally — no round-trip to an auth server. Claims like `exp`, `aud`, and `scope` are embedded. Stateless validation is the key REST-friendly property here.

**OAuth2** adds a delegation layer. A REST client exchanges credentials at a token endpoint (`/oauth/token`) and receives a short-lived access token (usually a JWT) plus a refresh token. The `scope` claim restricts which REST endpoints the token can reach.

**mTLS** authenticates at the TLS layer — the client presents a certificate alongside the server cert. REST sees the verified `CN` or SAN via a forwarded header like `X-Client-Cert-Subject`. No `Authorization` header needed; identity is transport-bound.
bytes.code_snippet:
# OAuth2 Client Credentials flow against a REST API
import httpx

TOKEN_URL = "https://auth.example.com/oauth/token"
API_BASE  = "https://api.example.com"

def get_access_token(client_id: str, client_secret: str) -> str:
    resp = httpx.post(TOKEN_URL, data={
        "grant_type": "client_credentials",
        "client_id": client_id,
        "client_secret": client_secret,
        "scope": "bytes:read bytes:write",
    })
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["access_token"]

token = get_access_token("my-client", "s3cr3t")
resp  = httpx.get(f"{API_BASE}/bytes",
                  headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"})
print(resp.json())

bytes.topic: Rate Limiting — per endpoint, per user, sliding window vs token bucket
bytes.title: REST Rate Limiting — Sliding Window vs Token Bucket Per Endpoint, Per User
bytes.body:
REST APIs expose rate limiting via response headers: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`, and `Retry-After` on 429s. How you enforce those limits internally changes behavior dramatically.

Token bucket allows bursting: a user can fire 20 requests instantly if they've accumulated tokens, then refill at 5 req/s. Ideal for `POST /uploads` or `POST /ai/generate` — bursty by nature. Sliding window is stricter: it tracks request timestamps in a rolling window, so 100 req/min means no more than 100 in any 60-second slice, not just per clock-minute. Preferred for `GET /feed` or `GET /search` to prevent thundering-herd abuse.

Per-endpoint granularity matters. A REST API should never apply one global limiter — `POST /auth/login` deserves aggressive limits (5/min per IP), while `GET /users/{id}` can afford 300/min per authenticated user. Attach limiters at the route level, keyed on `user_id` from the JWT or IP for unauthenticated routes. Store state in Redis with atomic Lua scripts to avoid race conditions across replicas.

bytes.topic: Pagination — cursor vs offset, total counts, infinite scroll patterns
bytes.title: REST Pagination — Cursor vs Offset, Total Counts, and Infinite Scroll
bytes.body: Offset pagination (`GET /posts?page=2&limit=20`) is simple but breaks under concurrent writes — items shift between pages as new rows are inserted. Cursor pagination (`GET /posts?after=eyJpZCI6MTAwfQ&limit=20`) uses an opaque pointer (often a base64-encoded `{id, timestamp}`) tied to a stable sort key, making it immune to drift. For infinite scroll in REST APIs, return a `next_cursor` in the response envelope alongside the page data; a `null` cursor signals the end of the collection. Avoid returning `total_count` by default — it requires a full `COUNT(*)` query that doesn't benefit from your cursor index. If clients need it (e.g., for a progress indicator), expose it as an opt-in query param: `?include_total=true`. Use `Link` headers (RFC 5988) alongside the body envelope for machine-readable navigation. Keep cursors short-lived (15–60 min TTL) if they encode sensitive sort criteria, and never expose raw row IDs in cursors — always encode them.

bytes.topic: Filtering & Sorting — query param conventions, GraphQL args, protobuf fields
bytes.title: REST API Filtering & Sorting — Query Param Conventions That Scale
bytes.body:
REST has no spec for filtering and sorting, so conventions vary wildly. The most widely adopted pattern uses bracket notation for filters and a `sort` param with prefixed direction: `GET /articles?filter[status]=published&filter[author]=42&sort=-createdAt,title`. The `-` prefix signals descending; no prefix means ascending. This mirrors JSON:API's convention and is unambiguous without needing a separate `order` param.

For range filters, use suffixed operators: `filter[views][gte]=1000&filter[views][lte]=5000`. Avoid encoding operators in values (`views=gte:1000`) — that breaks typed parsing and URL validation.

Pagination belongs alongside: `page[number]=2&page[size]=25` keeps cursor and offset strategies swappable without breaking client contracts.

On the server, never interpolate query params into raw SQL. Map allowed filter fields to a whitelist and use parameterized queries. Expose only indexed columns as sortable — an unindexed `sort=-body` on a large table is a full table scan waiting to happen.

bytes.topic: Caching — HTTP cache headers, CDN, ETag and conditional requests
bytes.title: REST Caching — HTTP Cache Headers, ETags, and CDN Strategy
bytes.body: REST APIs leverage HTTP's native caching model through response headers. `Cache-Control: max-age=3600, public` tells CDNs and browsers to cache GET responses for an hour without revalidation. For mutable resources, use `Cache-Control: no-cache` paired with an `ETag` — the server returns a hash of the resource body (e.g. `ETag: "a3f4b2c1"`). Clients store it and send `If-None-Match: "a3f4b2c1"` on subsequent requests. If unchanged, the server returns `304 Not Modified` with no body, saving bandwidth. For time-based validation, `Last-Modified` and `If-Modified-Since` serve the same purpose but are less precise. On CDN edges (Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront), `Vary: Accept-Encoding, Authorization` prevents serving cached authenticated responses to unauthenticated users — critical for REST endpoints mixing public and private data. Avoid `Cache-Control: no-store` unless the response contains sensitive data; it disables caching entirely, killing CDN offload rates. Design your REST resource URLs to be cache-key-friendly: stable, canonical, and free of session-specific query params.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — consistent error format across protocols, problem+json
bytes.title: REST Error Handling — Consistent Responses with problem+json (RFC 7807)
bytes.body: Ad-hoc error responses break API consumers. RFC 7807 defines `application/problem+json` — a standardized error envelope that every REST client can parse uniformly. The spec mandates five fields: `type` (URI identifying the error class), `title` (human-readable summary), `status` (HTTP status code mirrored in the body), `detail` (instance-specific explanation), and `instance` (URI of the specific occurrence). ASP.NET Core's `ProblemDetails` implements this out of the box — call `Results.Problem()` or `Results.ValidationProblem()` in Minimal APIs and the correct `Content-Type: application/problem+json` header is set automatically. Extend the base object with domain-specific fields (e.g. `traceId`, `errors` map for validation) by subclassing `ProblemDetails`. On the client side, inspect `Content-Type` before parsing — a `problem+json` response signals a structured error regardless of status code, letting you display `detail` directly or log `instance` for support. Never mix plain `application/json` error shapes with `problem+json` on the same API — pick one contract and enforce it via a global exception handler.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — safe vs idempotent methods, idempotency keys for mutations
bytes.title: REST Idempotency — Safe vs Idempotent Methods and Mutation Keys
bytes.body:
RFC 9110 draws a sharp line between safe and idempotent. Safe methods (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS) produce no server-side state changes — clients can call them freely. Idempotent methods (PUT, DELETE, and all safe methods) guarantee that N identical requests leave the same server state as one — no guarantees about response codes, just the outcome. POST is neither: two identical POST /orders requests can create two orders.

The practical problem: networks are unreliable. A POST that times out may have already succeeded server-side, so a retry doubles the mutation. The fix is an idempotency key — a client-generated UUID sent in a header (commonly Idempotency-Key, as Stripe and Adyen standardize). The server caches the response against that key (typically in Redis with a TTL) and replays it on duplicate requests without re-executing the business logic.

PUT is naturally idempotent because it replaces a resource wholesale. PATCH is not guaranteed idempotent — increment-style patches ("add 1 to quantity") are sensitive to retries. Design PATCH bodies as full-replacement diffs or apply idempotency keys to them explicitly.

bytes.topic: Security — input validation, CSRF, injection, GraphQL introspection risks
bytes.title: REST API Security — Validation, CSRF, and Injection Defense in Practice
bytes.body:
REST APIs expose attack surface at every route boundary. Three patterns eliminate the most critical vectors.

**Input Validation:** Use Pydantic (FastAPI) or Joi (Express) to enforce strict field constraints before business logic runs. Whitelist with regex patterns — blocklists miss edge cases. Set `model_config = ConfigDict(extra='forbid')` to reject unknown fields entirely; never silently ignore them.

**CSRF:** REST APIs using `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>` are inherently CSRF-immune — browsers cannot attach custom headers cross-origin via form submissions. If you must use cookies, set `SameSite=Strict; Secure; HttpOnly` and validate `Origin`/`Referer` server-side. Never treat `Content-Type: application/json` as a CSRF defense — it isn't one.

**Injection:** Parameterized queries are non-negotiable. Never interpolate path params or query strings into SQL. For MongoDB, enforce `$eq` operators and block `$where` at the schema layer. Sanitize path parameters that touch the filesystem or shell.

Disable `docs_url` and `redoc_url` in production FastAPI — your OpenAPI schema is the REST equivalent of GraphQL introspection, and leaking it hands attackers a full route map.
bytes.code_snippet:
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic import constr
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

app = FastAPI(docs_url=None, redoc_url=None)  # no schema in prod

class CreateUserPayload(BaseModel):
    model_config = {"extra": "forbid"}  # reject unknown fields
    username: constr(pattern=r'^[a-zA-Z0-9_]{3,30}$')
    email: str = Field(..., pattern=r'^[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[a-z]{2,}$')
    age: int = Field(..., ge=13, le=120)

@app.post("/users")
async def create_user(payload: CreateUserPayload, db=Depends(get_db)):
    # Parameterized — never f-string or .format() into SQL
    row = await db.fetchrow(
        "INSERT INTO users (username, email, age) VALUES ($1, $2, $3) RETURNING id",
        payload.username, payload.email, payload.age,
    )
    return {"id": row["id"]}

bytes.topic: Performance — payload size, compression, connection reuse, multiplexing
bytes.title: REST API Performance — Compression, Keep-Alive, and HTTP/2 Multiplexing
bytes.body: Every REST response travels over the wire, so payload size and connection overhead compound fast at scale. Enable gzip or Brotli compression on your server — most clients send `Accept-Encoding: gzip, br` automatically, and compressing JSON typically yields 60–80% size reduction. On the client, avoid creating a new TCP connection per request: HTTP/1.1 Keep-Alive reuses the socket, but you still queue requests serially. HTTP/2 solves this with multiplexing — multiple in-flight REST requests share a single TCP connection via independent streams, eliminating head-of-line blocking. On the server, trim response payloads with sparse fieldsets: expose a `?fields=id,name,status` query param and strip unused keys before serialization. For repeated read-heavy endpoints, set `Cache-Control: max-age` and return `ETag` headers — clients send `If-None-Match` and get a 304 with zero body bytes when nothing changed. Combine these: compressed, cached, multiplexed REST calls over HTTP/2 with field-filtered payloads can reduce bandwidth 5–10x and latency by 40% versus naive HTTP/1.1 with full payloads.

bytes.topic: Testing — contract testing, mocking, integration tests per protocol
bytes.title: REST Contract Testing with Pact — Catch API Breaks Before They Hit Prod
bytes.body:
REST APIs break silently — a provider renames a field, the consumer keeps expecting the old shape, and nothing catches it until production. Pact solves this with consumer-driven contract testing: the consumer defines exactly which request/response pairs it relies on, publishes a pact file, and the provider verifies it independently.

On the consumer side, Pact spins up a local mock server. You define interactions describing the HTTP method, path, and the specific fields your code actually uses — not the full response shape. This generates a pact JSON file encoding the contract.

On the provider side, `pact.verifyProvider()` replays each interaction against real handler code and checks responses match. No shared staging environment needed — each side tests independently.

For integration tests, keep scope narrow: one endpoint per test, real HTTP client (`supertest` in Node, `httpx` in Python), seeded database. Assert on status codes, `Content-Type` headers, and response shape via JSON Schema or Zod. Mock only at the HTTP boundary using MSW or WireMock — never mock your own service internals, which hides the contract drift you're trying to catch.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Protocol — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC decision framework
bytes.title: REST — Choosing Between REST, GraphQL, and gRPC: A Decision Framework
bytes.body:
REST shines when HTTP semantics do real work: cache-control headers, ETags, and CDN edge caching are free wins you lose with GraphQL's POST-only model or gRPC's binary framing. Stick with REST when you're building public APIs where broad client compatibility matters, or when your resources map cleanly to nouns with standard CRUD verbs.

Switch to GraphQL when multiple clients (mobile, web, third-party) need different shapes of the same data — overfetching a user object 50 fields wide on a mobile client is a REST-native pain point GraphQL solves directly.

Reach for gRPC when you control both ends of the wire: internal service-to-service calls where Protobuf's binary encoding and HTTP/2 multiplexing reduce latency meaningfully, or when you need bidirectional streaming that REST's request-response model can't express.

The tell: if you're adding `?fields=id,name` query params to trim response size, you've outgrown REST for that endpoint. If you're hand-rolling retry logic over HTTP/1.1 between services, gRPC's built-in interceptors and status codes are worth the schema overhead.
